title: Tibetan Kangyur Editions of the Heart Sūtra
description: Comparison of the Heart Sūtra across major Kangyur editions. The Tibetan translation was made in the 8th-9th century from Sanskrit. Both short and long recensions exist in the Kangyur. Sigla follow Silk (1994).
source: Silk, Jonathan A. (1994). The Heart Sūtra in Tibetan: A Critical Edition of the Two Recensions Contained in the Kanjur. Wiener Studien zur Tibetologie und Buddhismuskunde 34. Vienna.

editions.silk_siglum: D (D1 = Prajñāpāramitā section, Toh 21; D2 = Tantra section, Toh 531)
editions.volumes: D1: shes rab, ka, 144.b.6-146.a.2; D2: rgyud, na (Tantra section)
editions.authority: Standard reference edition used by Western scholars. Silk (1994) uses D as base text. Both D1 and D2 are Recension A texts.
editions.notes: The Degé contains the Heart Sūtra twice: in Prajñāpāramitā (D1) and Tantra (D2) sections. Both are Recension A texts, virtually identical. Silk observes that this is because the Degé editors added a copy of their Tantra text to the Prajñāpāramitā section (pp. 27). D is stemmatically a copy of J ('Phying ba stag rtse), with contamination from the Them spangs ma line (Silk stemma, p. 21).

editions.catalog: K/Q: rgyud, na, 39.b.7-41.a.5; P: rgyud, na, 39.b.7-41.a.5
editions.location: Beijing, commissioned by Qing Emperor Kangxi
editions.date_note: Silk (1994, p. 89) dates K to 1692. P is the 1737 reprint/revision. The text upon which the Tokyo reprint is based is the 1720 edition (called Q by Eimer); the Paris edition is the 1737 print (p. 95).
editions.authority: Important alternate edition. Silk's siglum K refers to the 1692 edition (hand-copy from Harvard-Yenching Institute), while P refers to the 1737 xylograph.
editions.notes: The Peking Kangyur preserves only the Tantra section text (Recension A). The Peking line (K, P) descends from the Wanli (1606) and ultimately the Yongle (1410) editions, in the Tshal pa textual lineage. K contains hand corrections. Folio 40 of the Harvard K copy is missing (Silk p. 95).

editions.silk_siglum: N (N1 = Prajñāpāramitā section; N2 = Tantra section)
editions.catalog: N1: sna tshogs, ka, 262.b.1-264.a.4; N2: rgyud, da, 92.a.4-94.a.2
editions.location: Narthang Monastery, near Shigatse, central Tibet
editions.authority: Important Gelug-affiliated edition from the Them spangs ma / Tshal pa mixed tradition
editions.recension: long (N1 = Recension A; N2 = Recension A)
editions.notes: Both N1 and N2 are Recension A. The Narthang, completed in two separate periods, contains texts from both the Them spangs ma and Tshal pa lines depending on the section (Silk p. 26). All exemplars are very difficult to read (Silk p. 92-93).

editions.silk_siglum: H (H1 = Prajñāpāramitā section, Recension B; H2 = Tantra section, Recension A)
editions.catalog: H1: sna tshogs (Prajñāpāramitā); H2: rgyud, tha, 45.a.1-47.a.2
editions.volumes: H1: found in H.5933; H2: found in H.5987
editions.authority: Latest major Kangyur edition. H1 is notable as a Recension B text with heavy conflation from Recension A.
editions.recension: long (H1 = Recension B with Rec A conflation; H2 = Recension A)
editions.notes: The Lhasa Kangyur is stemmatically a copy of N (Narthang) for most texts, with contamination from D. H1 is one of the few Recension B witnesses in the Prajñāpāramitā section, though it shows considerable conflation with Recension A (Silk pp. 26, 88). H2 is a standard Recension A text.

editions.location: Lithang, Kham (also known as 'Jang sa tham)
editions.date_note: Imaeda suggested 1608-1621; Silk notes this as 'Phying ba stag rtse in the stemma. The date is between 1609 and 1614 per Samten (1987).
editions.authority: Earliest datable printed Kangyur. Source for D (Degé) and C (Cone). J is a Tshal pa / 'Phying ba stag rtse text.
editions.notes: The legibility of copies available to Silk was very poor (p. 89). J is the ancestor of D and C in the Tshal pa / 'Phying ba stag rtse line. It preserves some readings older than D, as D was later contaminated by Them spangs ma readings (Silk pp. 23, 89).

editions.silk_siglum: R (R1 = Prajñāpāramitā section; R2 = Tantra section)
editions.catalog: R1: sras sna tshogs, ka, 277.a.2-278.b.5; R2: rgyud, da, 94.a.5-95.b.7
editions.date_note: Silk (1994, p. 96) lists the date as 'Unknown' for both R1 and R2. The reprint edition was published in Leh, 1979 (vol. 51, #13). The manuscript is from the Them spangs ma line but cannot be securely placed in the stemma.
editions.authority: One of the most important Kanjurs for text criticism. Preserves a Them spangs ma text that is somewhat independent of the main stemma.
editions.recension: long (R1 = Recension A; R2 = Recension A)
editions.notes: S (as Silk calls the Phu brag manuscript, not Stog) is somewhat difficult to place stemmatically. Silk places it floating near Recension A in his stemma but not firmly attached to any branch (p. 26). R1 and R2 preserve separate recensions without apparent editorial leveling (Silk p. 26). The Tantra section of the Phu brag (S) apparently does not contain the Heart Sūtra (Silk p. 27). The tsheg before the shad is found only after nga (Silk p. 96).

editions.location: Library of Tibetan Works and Archives, Dharamsala (from IASWR microfiche)
editions.date_note: Silk (1994, p. 97) gives 1696-1706, per Samten (1989). The manuscript seems very well written.
editions.authority: Important independent Them spangs ma witness. Appears only in the Prajñāpāramitā section, unlike most Kangyurs which have it in Tantra or both.
editions.recension: long (Recension A, with some distinctive readings)
editions.notes: Silk uses the siglum S for this manuscript. It is the only Kangyur available to Silk that has the Heart Sūtra only in the Prajñāpāramitā section and not in the Tantra section (p. 27). S preserves important independent readings and cannot be firmly placed in the stemma; Silk represents it as floating near Recension A (p. 21 stemma). The microfiche quality is very poor (p. 97).

siglum_concordance.note: Concordance between sigla used in this dataset and Silk (1994). Silk uses different sigla from some other reference systems.

short_recension.note: The Tibetan tradition preserves both long and short recensions. The short recension lacks the frame narrative. Silk calls these Recension A (Tantra section / Tshal pa line, typically) and Recension B (Prajñāpāramitā section / Them spangs ma line). Both are long-form texts with frame narratives; the 'short recension' without frame narrative is found only in Dunhuang manuscripts and commentaries, not in printed Kangyur editions.
short_recension.clarification: Silk's Recension A vs Recension B distinction is NOT the same as short vs long recension. Both Rec A and Rec B in the Kangyur are 'long' texts with frame narratives. They differ in specific readings (e.g., invocation formula, verb forms, word order). The truly 'short' recension (without frame narrative) is preserved in Dunhuang manuscripts like IOL Tib J 751 and in Kamalaśīla's commentary.
short_recension.relationship_to_long: The short recension corresponds more closely to T251 (Chinese) and the Hōryūji manuscript (Sanskrit). The long recension adds the frame narrative found in Sanskrit long recension and T257.

variant_readings.methodology: Key orthographic and substantive variants between Kangyur editions. Base text: Degé (D). Variants documented from Silk (1994) critical apparatus.

variants.degé: བྷ་ག་བ་ཏཱི་པྲཛྙཱ་པཱ་ར་མི་ཏཱ་ཧྲྀ་ད་ཡ (bha ga ba tI praj+nyA pA ra mi tA h+rI da ya)
variants.note: Recension A has bha ga ba ti (variants: CDJU read ti; BFKPs read ta). Recension B has ārya bha ga ba ti. D1 reads h+rI (not hri), matching the 'Phying ba stag rtse texts.

variants.recension_A: བཅོམ་ལྡན་འདས་མ་ཤེས་རབ་ཀྱི་ཕ་རོལ་ཏུ་ཕྱིན་པ་ལ་ཕྱག་འཚལ་ལོ། (bcom ldan 'das ma shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa la phyag 'tshal lo)
variants.recension_B: སངས་རྒྱས་དང་བྱང་ཆུབ་སེམས་དཔའ་ཐམས་ཅད་ལ་ཕྱག་འཚལ་ལོ། (sangs rgyas dang byang chub sems dpa' thams cad la phyag 'tshal lo)
variants.note: The invocations differ between recensions. Rec A addresses Bhagavatī Prajñāpāramitā; Rec B addresses all buddhas and bodhisattvas. This corresponds to different Sanskrit namo formulae (Silk p. 33). The old JSON incorrectly had the Rec B form for Degé.

variants.recension_A: rgyal po'i khab ... (with na inserted by D1, J, U1 after khab; shad inserted by most Them spangs ma texts)
variants.recension_B: rgyal po'i khab na ... (with na and shad)
variants.note: D1 inserts na after khab, agreeing with Recension B (Silk para D, note 2). The 'Phying ba stag rtse texts (C, D1, J, U1) share this reading.

Both recensions read sangs rgyas kyi mthus (through the power of the Buddha). The Sanskritized form bud+d+has stobs kyis does NOT appear in any Kangyur witness collated by Silk. The old JSON incorrectly had bud+d+has stobs kyis.

variants.recension_A: ཟབ་མོ་སྤྱོད་པ (zab mo spyod pa, without genitive 'i)
variants.recension_B: ཟབ་མོ་སྤྱོད་པ (zab mo spyod pa, without genitive 'i; S reads mo 'i)
variants.note: Both recensions lack the genitive 'i between zab mo and spyod pa in most witnesses. Only S (Phu brag) reads mo 'i. The old JSON incorrectly had zab mo'i.
Rec A reads de lta bas na; Rec B reads de ltar. S reads ltar, corresponding to Rec B. The old JSON had de ltar for the Degé text, which was incorrect.
Rec A reads skrag pa; Rec B reads 'jigs pa. Both translate Sanskrit trāsa/trasta. The old JSON incorrectly had 'jigs pa for the Degé. U2 may read sgrag? (Silk p. 132 note l).

variants.recension_A_base: ཏདྱ་ཐཱ། ཨོཾ་ག་ཏེ་ག་ཏེ ... (tadya thā / oṃ ga te ga te ...)
variants.D1_reading: ཏདྱ་ཐཱ། ག་ཏེ་ག་ཏེ ... (tadya thā / ga te ga te ..., NO oṃ)
variants.recension_B: ཏདྱ་ཐཱ། ག་ཏེ་ག་ཏེ ... (tad ya thā / ga te ga te ..., NO oṃ)
variants.note: The Rec A base text includes oṃ, but D1 individually omits it. Silk (p. 138, note 1): 'C, D1, D2, H2, J, U1, U2: Omit oṃ, agreeing with Recension B.' This means D1 agrees with Rec B on this point, while the Rec A base (B, F, K, P) retains oṃ. The old JSON incorrectly included oṃ in the Degé mantra.
Rec A places de ltar before shes rab; Rec B places it after zab mo la. The old JSON had the Rec B word order.
All editions use zad pa (exhaustion/kṣaya) rather than a term for nirodha. This matches Sanskrit kṣaya.
Tibetan has 5 epithets, while Chinese T251 has 4 and T250 has 3.

translation_colophon.revisers: Gewé Lo (དགེ་བློ) and Namkha (ནམ་མཁའ) et al.
translation_colophon.note: The colophon credits these translators and revisers. Silk (1994, pp. 47-56) discusses the colophon in detail. The translation was attributed to Vimalamitra, who is thought to have arrived in Tibet around 797 CE (Horiuchi 2022). The revised Kangyur text reflects the 'new, fixed language' (skad gsar bcad) of the second resolution (c. 815 CE).

stemmatic_position.summary: The Tibetan translation was made from Sanskrit, not Chinese. It therefore represents a witness to the Sanskrit tradition, not an independent transmission line.
stemmatic_position.relationship_to_sanskrit: The Tibetan long recension corresponds to the Sanskrit long recension (with frame narrative). The Tibetan short recension (Dunhuang manuscripts only) corresponds to the Sanskrit short recension.
stemmatic_position.relationship_to_chinese: The Tibetan is a secondary witness for establishing the original Chinese text, mediated through Sanskrit.
value_for_text_criticism: Tibetan zad pa matches Sanskrit kṣaya, confirming back-translation from Chinese
D1 omits oṃ from mantra, agreeing with Rec B and some Sanskrit witnesses
Tibetan preserves frame narrative matching Sanskrit long recension
Tibetan ma thob pa (no non-attainment) reflects Sanskrit na-aprāptiḥ interpolation
Two Tibetan recensions (A and B) likely reflect two different Sanskrit exemplars (Silk pp. 31-48)

scholarly_refs: Silk, Jonathan A. (1994). 'The Heart Sūtra in Tibetan: A Critical Edition of the Two Recensions Contained in the Kanjur.' Wiener Studien zur Tibetologie und Buddhismuskunde 34. Vienna.

Apple, James B. (2024). 'Kamalaśīla's Word Commentary to the Heart Sūtra, Translation and Edition.' Revue d'Études Tibétaines, no. 70, pp. 5-82.
Harrison, Paul (1996). 'A Brief History of the Tibetan bKa' 'gyur.' In Tibetan Literature: Studies in Genre, pp. 70-94.
Eimer, Helmut (1992). Der Tantra-Teil des Kanjur. Arbeitskreis für Tibetische und Buddhistische Studien.
Samten, Jampa (1987). 'Notes on the Lithang Edition of the Tibetan bKa'-'gyur.' Tibet Journal 12(3): 17-40.
